segments.label: Compute & Hardware
segments.description: GPU, accelerator, cluster, and low-level systems suppliers.

segments.label: Semiconductor Supply Chain
segments.description: Foundries, memory, advanced packaging, lithography, inspection, and semiconductor equipment upstream of AI accelerators.

segments.label: Datacenter, Power & Cooling
segments.description: Sites, interconnection, power distribution, backup generation, thermal management, and energy procurement for AI clusters.

segments.label: Industrial Materials
segments.description: Wafers, specialty gases, copper, rare earths, water, chemicals, and other physical inputs that constrain AI buildout.

segments.label: Cloud AI Platforms
segments.description: Hyperscaler and neocloud platforms that package compute, storage, managed models, and deployment.

segments.label: Foundation Model Labs
segments.description: Closed and open frontier model suppliers across text, code, audio, image, and video.

segments.label: Open Model Ops
segments.description: Open-weight distribution, local runtime, fine-tuning, quantization, and model packaging.

segments.label: Data, Context & Labeling
segments.description: Labeling, document parsing, synthetic data, ETL, and context preparation.

segments.label: Retrieval & Memory
segments.description: Vector search, hybrid retrieval, knowledge graphs, memory, and personalization stores.

segments.label: Agents & Orchestration
segments.description: Frameworks and runtimes for tool use, workflows, browser/computer use, and multi-agent systems.

segments.label: Serving, Inference & Routing
segments.description: Inference servers, model gateways, routing, caching, batching, and cost/latency optimization.

segments.label: Evals, Observability & Testing
segments.description: Traces, evals, regression tests, red teams, feedback loops, and quality monitoring.

segments.label: Security, Safety & Governance
segments.description: Prompt-injection defense, AI firewall, DLP, model risk, policy, audit, and compliance.

segments.label: Application Infrastructure
segments.description: Developer-facing platforms and vertical agent shells that turn AI components into deployable products.

layers.label: L0 - Industrial Inputs
layers.description: Power, fabs, packaging, cooling, sites, grid interconnects, materials, and other non-software bottlenecks.

layers.label: L1 - Physical Compute
layers.description: Chips, servers, accelerators, clusters, and scheduler primitives.

layers.label: L2 - Model Supply
layers.description: Frontier models, open weights, fine-tuned models, embeddings, and multimodal models.

layers.label: L3 - Data / Context
layers.description: The data, retrieval, memory, and tool context models need to act usefully.

layers.label: L4 - Orchestration / Runtime
layers.description: Agent loops, workflow graphs, tool calling, gateways, and application runtime.

layers.label: L5 - Serving / Inference
layers.description: Inference APIs, model servers, gateways, routing, caching, batching, and latency/cost controls.

layers.label: L6 - Evals / Observability
layers.description: Traces, evals, regression tests, feedback, experiments, and quality monitoring.

layers.label: L7 - Security / Governance
layers.description: Safety, policy, DLP, model risk, audit, compliance, and production controls.

layers.label: L8 - Application Layer
layers.description: User-facing copilots, coding agents, voice agents, enterprise automation, and vertical workflows.

verticals.label: Simulation / Autonomy Tooling
verticals.description: Simulation, synthetic data, scenario generation, validation, safety cases, and autonomy development tooling.

verticals.label: Logistics / Supply Chain
verticals.description: Freight, delivery, warehouse flow, fleet operations, routing, and supply-chain automation.

verticals.label: Construction / Real Estate
verticals.description: Construction automation, reality capture, asset inspection, property operations, and built-world workflows.

verticals.label: Creative / Media
verticals.description: Generated images, video, audio, design, publishing, and media workflows.

verticals.label: Defense / Security
verticals.description: National security, cybersecurity, model-weight protection, and critical infrastructure.

verticals.label: Consumer
verticals.description: Personal assistants, chat, on-device AI, and consumer-facing apps.

verticals.label: Edge / Device

verticals.label: Multi-Vertical
verticals.description: Horizontal infrastructure serving many buyer markets.

cost_tiers.label: Free
cost_tiers.description: Useful no-cost tier or open-source project.

cost_tiers.label: Freemium
cost_tiers.description: Free tier with paid scale, hosted usage, or team features.

cost_tiers.label: Paid
cost_tiers.description: Self-serve paid product or usage-based API.

cost_tiers.label: Enterprise
cost_tiers.description: Negotiated pricing, committed capacity, or sales-led deployment.

structural.reason: Model labs consume clusters; when they own datacenters, the entity is still classified by model supply unless it sells compute.
Labeling and context companies operate above storage/compute; their bottleneck is data rights and workflow, not accelerator supply.
Retrieval systems run on compute, but they are not compute infrastructure.
Evals and traces measure systems; they do not provide accelerator capacity.
Governance products sit in the control plane, not the hardware plane.
Datacenter and power providers enable model supply but do not supply trained model weights or APIs.
Materials companies supply fabs, grids, and construction; they do not package model capability.
Chip and cluster suppliers enable model training but are not model suppliers unless they expose models as the product.
Safety vendors may use models internally, but their product is policy/control, not model supply.
Hardware has no user-level context semantics.
Materials are physical supply constraints, not model context or memory systems.
Semiconductor suppliers shape the physical frontier; they do not sell end-user AI applications.
Power, cooling, and sites remain industrial infrastructure even when their buyers are AI applications.
Wafers, gases, metals, and water are input markets, not application-layer products.
Compute becomes visible through clouds or apps; chip vendors are not application infrastructure.

opportunity.note: Contested: Okta, Microsoft Agent 365, WorkOS, and enterprise IAM/security suites now cover agent identity, lifecycle, and scoped authorization. The remaining gap is cross-vendor signed action receipts and delegated-intent policy across Okta, Entra, MCP servers, browsers, and SaaS tools.
Open: portable long-term memory that survives model/app switches, supports user consent, and avoids becoming another silo owned by one assistant vendor.
Open: an inference broker with neutral routing, audit, fallback, and spend governance across closed, open, hosted, and self-hosted models.
Open: accepted eval standards for vertical agents. Coding has SWE-bench-style signals; support, sales, legal, finance, and ops agents still lack trusted task suites.

Partial: GridCARE is opening the utility-side time-to-power market, while IREN, Core Scientific, TeraWulf, Riot, CleanSpark, Bitfarms/Keel, and Hut 8 are converting power-rich mining sites into AI/HPC supply. Still open: neutral technical diligence, brokerage, and operating standards for miner-to-GPU cluster conversions.
Open: trusted demand and provenance rails for wafers, gases, copper, water, construction inputs, and critical minerals tied to AI cluster buildout.

legend_flows.description: Two flows run through AI infrastructure. SUPPLY flows upward from chips and clusters to model providers, serving systems, and applications. CONTROL flows back down through context, evals, security, policy, routing, and observability. The durable companies tend to own a choke point in one flow and sell trust into the other.
legend_flows.scope_note: Pure AI application categories remain out of scope unless they sell reusable infrastructure, but vertical-facing slices are tracked so finance, defense, healthcare, energy, semis, and industrial constraints can be inspected across the stack.

field_notes.country: Optional ISO-3166 alpha-2 HQ country, shown on cards and searchable. Region remains the facet.
field_notes.subcomponents: Use tags and notes for sub-components; top-level matrix columns stay broad enough to remain readable.
